title: Make a Thing That Works
developmental_frame: At 11–12, children can follow a multi-step plan and love making something *real* appear from their own effort, but they get discouraged fast when a first attempt is messy. They don't need programming syntax; they need the experience of turning an idea into a working thing and seeing a real person use it. Keep the build small enough to finish in a sitting or two so the payoff arrives before the patience runs out.
authentic_value_meaning: Value at this age is: 'I made this, and it actually works, and someone used it.' The thrill is the gap between an idea in your head and a real thing on a screen that does something. Usefulness to one real person beats polish.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child builds the smallest possible working version of something useful — a quiz for a friend, a tiny web page for a family event, a simple automation — and watches a real person use it. The magic of 'the thing I imagined now exists and works' is the driver, sharpened by one real user actually trying it. Finishing something real is the reward.

artifact: One small working digital thing (a quiz, a one-page site, a simple automation) that does something useful, tried at least once by a real person who isn't the builder.
success_criteria: The thing runs and does what it's meant to for a real user.
The child can explain in plain words what each part does — nothing in it is a mystery to them.
They changed at least one thing after watching a real person get confused by it.

lessons.title: Who is it for, and what does it do?
lessons.concept: Before building anything, name the one person it's for and the one thing it does for them. A builder who skips this makes something cool that nobody needs.
lessons.real_example: 'A quiz to help my little brother practise his times tables' beats 'a maths app'. One person, one job — now you know exactly what to build.
lessons.apply_task: Write one sentence: 'This is for [name], and it helps them [do one thing].' If you can't, your idea isn't ready yet.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Is the one thing small enough to actually finish? Beginners always pick too big — what could you cut?
lessons.feedback_task: Tell your chosen person your sentence and ask: 'Would that actually help you?' Listen for a real yes.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a friend write their own 'for [name], helps them [do X]' sentence.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Fine to brainstorm ideas with AI, but *you* pick the real person and the real job — the model doesn't know who's actually in your life to help.
success_criteria: A one-person, one-job project sentence is written and checked with the real person.

lessons.title: Sketch before you build
lessons.concept: Draw the thing on paper first — what's on the screen, what happens when you tap. A sketch is cheap to change; a half-built project is painful to change.
lessons.real_example: Game designers draw levels on paper before touching a computer. Ten seconds with a pencil can save an hour of building the wrong thing.
lessons.apply_task: Sketch every screen or step of your thing on paper, with arrows showing what happens next. Keep it rough.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Walking through your own sketch, where does it get confusing? Better to find it now, on paper.
lessons.feedback_task: Show the sketch to your real user and have them 'tap' through it with a finger. Note where they hesitate.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone why sketching first saves time, using your own before/after.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Sketch by hand yourself — this is thinking, not decoration. AI can't do your thinking about what the thing should be; that has to live in your own head first.
success_criteria: A hand sketch shows every screen/step with the flow between them.

lessons.title: Build the smallest version
lessons.concept: Build the tiniest version that works at all — one question, one page, one action. You can always add more, but only once the small thing runs. Understand every piece you put in.
lessons.real_example: The first version of a big app is usually embarrassingly basic — one screen that barely works. That's correct. Small-and-working beats big-and-broken every time.
lessons.apply_task: Build the smallest working version from your sketch, using whatever tool fits (a no-code builder, a simple site maker, AI help). Get it to actually run.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Can you explain what every part does? Anything you can't explain is a part you don't yet control.
lessons.feedback_task: Show a mentor or parent the running thing and have them point to any part that looks like a 'mystery box' to you. Go understand that part.
lessons.teach_back_task: Walk someone through your build and explain what each piece does in plain words.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help you build — but the rule is: **never ship what you can't explain.** If AI made a piece you don't understand, either learn it or take it out. A builder who can't explain their own thing isn't really building, they're copying.
assets_needed: List of kid-safe no-code / site-builder tools
success_criteria: A minimal version runs and does its one job.
The child can explain every part of it in their own words.

lessons.title: Test it on a real person and fix one thing
lessons.concept: You can't see your own thing clearly — you know how it's *meant* to work, so you skip past the confusing bits. A real user finds them in seconds. Then you fix the worst one.
lessons.real_example: Watch someone use your quiz without helping them. Where they get stuck or press the wrong thing tells you exactly what to fix — no guessing needed.
lessons.apply_task: Hand your thing to your real user and stay quiet. Write down every place they get confused. Then fix the single most confusing one.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Was the confusing part the bit you were most proud of? That happens a lot — building for yourself vs building for them are different.
lessons.feedback_task: After fixing, have them try again. Did the fix work? If not, you found the wrong thing to fix.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach a friend the rule: 'watch them use it, stay silent, fix what confused them.'
lessons.ai_judgment_note: A real human tester beats any AI check here — AI won't get confused the way a real 8-year-old will. Use AI afterward only to help *fix* the specific thing your tester tripped on.
success_criteria: A real person tested the thing while the builder observed silently.
At least one change was made based on real confusion, then re-tested.
